scenes.narration: What if I told you every curve has a hidden speed at every single point?
Take this curve, f of x equals x squared. Watch what happens when we track the slope at every point.
See that green line? That's the tangent line. It shows the exact steepness of the curve right where the dot is. As the dot moves, the slope changes.
Right here at the bottom, the curve is completely flat. The slope is zero.
But over here, the curve is climbing fast. The slope is four. Same curve, totally different steepness.
So how do we find the exact slope at any point we want? That question has a name.
It's called the derivative. The derivative tells you the exact slope of a curve at a single point.

And it's everywhere. The slope of a position curve gives you velocity. Doctors use it to track how fast a tumor is growing. Businesses use it to find marginal cost. Physicists use it to get acceleration from velocity.
That's what a derivative is. In the next video, we'll show you exactly how to compute one.
